name: Michigan Advance
title: Mediator pauses negotiations between U-M and graduate employee union to map out bargaining details
description: A mediator appointed by the Michigan Employment Relations Commission has suspended contract negotiations between the University of Michigan and the Graduate Employees’ Organization for 28 days, calling for a “cooling off period.”  In a Feb. 12 letter, the mediator directed both sides to use the pause to coordinate on attendance limits, identification procedu[...]
text:
University of Michigan Central Campus sign | Photo by Jon King/Michigan AdvanceA mediator appointed by the Michigan Employment Relations Commission has suspended contract negotiations between the University of Michigan and the Graduate Employees’ Organization for 28 days, calling for a “cooling off period.” 
In a Feb. 12 letter, the mediator directed both sides to use the pause to coordinate on attendance limits, identification procedures, and other safety and security measures for future bargaining sessions. The university said it respects the commission’s authority, will comply with the directive, and remains committed to reaching a fair and sustainable contract.
“From the start of these negotiations last fall, the university has remained committed to productively engaging with GEO to reach agreement on a new contract that is fair for graduate student instructors and graduate student staff assistants and is sustainable for the university,” Alexandra Matish, associate vice provost and senior director of Academic Human Resources at the university, said in a statement.
Meanwhile, an agreement has been reached between the university and the union regarding the details of an election for research assistants to join the bargaining unit.
Research assistants will vote in a secret-ballot election governed by the Michigan Employment Relations Commission that will take place March 9 through March 25, with ballot counting for March 26. Ballots will be sent to the commission via mail.
A majority vote to be represented by the organization would ensure that the 2,400 graduate research assistants across all three U-M campuses would have a voice through the bargaining unit, which at present includes graduate student instructors and graduate student staff assistants.SUBSCRIBE: GET THE MORNING HEADLINES DELIVERED TO YOUR INBOX
The organization in a statement praised the agreement to hold an election as a monumental step, and was a result of years of grassroots organizing at the department level and a wide unionization card campaign.
“These cards were submitted to the Michigan Employment Relations Commission on December 10th, 2025. MERC informed us that those 1600 cards surpassed the 30% minimum threshold, officially securing an election for GSRA inclusion in GEO,” the organization said in a news release. “This overwhelming show of support highlights the desire for contractually guaranteed protections from issues graduate researchers face — from unfair dismissal and abusive advisor relations to loss of research funding and the incessant targeting of international workers.”
That said, the organization says it received pushback on the move.
“Despite the strong show of interest demonstrated by our petition, the University of Michigan responded by immediately calling our right to collective bargaining into question,” the organization said. “This was done by outright refusing to acknowledge Fellows as employees, stating they are merely ‘students receiving financial aid’ and claiming they cannot identify which grad workers are Fellows and thus cannot produce the list of eligible workers they are legally obligated to create. This was despite the fact that graduate workers systematically assembled a comparable list to aid our unionization effort, without having access to the university’s vast internal systems for disbursing salaries, health insurance, or grants.”
Although an election was agreed to, the organization claimed that U-M was prepared to force a legal battle if fellows were included as part of the union.
“This meant we were faced with an important moment of collective decision-making. Graduate workers across departments and job titles came together last month to determine which path would best enable us to fight for each other,” the organization said. “The question of Fellows’ unionization was one of when rather than if, given near unanimous agreement that Fellows are employees deserving of union rights. Over 600 graduate workers then voted on the researcher accretion timeline, with over 90% deciding to pursue GSRA unionization as soon as possible and continue fighting for Fellows as part of a future campaign.”
The organization said it would continue that fight as it celebrates some movement on, at least, the unionization of the graduate research assistants on U-M’s campuses.
Michigan Advance Editor-in-Chief Jon King contributed to this report.
